title: WHO IS DEAR TO GOD?  | Bhagavad Gita 12.16- 12.20 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
speaker: HG Amitasana Dasa

segments.text: The topic is, who is dear to Krishna? This is the last few verses, last six verses of the twelfth chapter of the Gita where Krishna is saying, who is dear to me?
How many of us want to be dear to Lord? Not that kind of, Bhagwan ko piyare ho gaye ho.
So it is used in the connotation of, he has reached God, he has died.
So Krishna is saying, they say, do not try to see the Lord, act in a way that Lord sees you.
Now Krishna is giving his opinion, who is very dear to me?
What can we do, what kind of person we can become or be, which will be very very pleasing to Krishna.
He will be very happy with us and such kind of person is very dear to him.
So this is the discussion which we are going to have today, who is dear to God?
We are covering sixteenth to twentieth verse of the twelfth chapter and this ends the twelfth chapter.
So we almost see the end of the twelfth chapter.
Sixty-six person through with the Gita and next week we will start with the thirteenth chapter
based on the teachings of Srila Prabhupada, our beloved spiritual master.
A devotee who is not dependent on the ordinary course of activities,
who is pure, expert, without cares, free from all pains,
and does not strive for some result, is very dear to me.
Now let us all understand that even in the twelfth chapter Krishna talks about different levels of devotion.
We have covered in the last two sessions.
Krishna says the highest is somebody who is always thinking of me.
Then he says, knowing that not everyone can fall in this bracket, not everyone can practice this.
He says, if you cannot do this, then try to practice the regulative principles
and develop a desire to attain me, to love me.
Then he further says, if you cannot do this, anyone can help me with an answer, if you cannot do this,
Krishna has a work, yada yadahi dharmasya glanir bhavati bharata.
Krishna is doing that work and his representatives are doing that work.
So we can do Krishna's work by ourselves being part of his mission
or helping others, those who are broadcasting his glories.
Then Krishna says, if you cannot do this, then try to be detached from the results of your activities
by offering, by using the results of your activities.
When we become attached to the results of our work, we get entangled.
When we work with the spirit of getting the results and enjoying them,
which is also called karma, lust, that is the sinful enemy of the living entity.
So he says, even if we cannot give it to Krishna, some people may not have that kind of inclination,
Okay, give it to some kind of charitable work.
Then if you cannot do that also, then Krishna says, you can do meditation.
There are lot of people who are doing meditation, meditation on void, meditation on some point, some candle light.
At least they have some desire to control the mind, senses.
And then Krishna says, if you cannot do this, just cultivate knowledge.
There are lot of philosophers who are trying to inquire, trying to figure out,
So here, what we are talking about is a devotee who is very exalted.
So what are his qualifications, what are his characteristics, what are his qualities?
He is not dependent on ordinary course of activities.
Who is pure, internally and externally, we will go one by one.
We all have lot of anxieties, lot of worries, heavy burden on our head.
So he is that way light, free from all pains, who does not strive for results.
We are always greedy for results, in anxiety.
But he is not attached to the results.
He is very dear to me.
Now pure, Prabhupada in the parbhupada explains, a devotee takes bath.
So purification is both internal and external.
Because we are not pure, shuchi or cleanliness is given lot of importance.
They say cleanliness is next to godliness.
So invariably in the temples, especially where deity worship is involved, a lot of prominence is given to cleanliness.
Even I have mentioned earlier, the devotees who go into the deity room, they have to invariably take bath and go.
If they have gone to clear their stomach or pass water, they have to take bath.
At least a brahmana who is trying to purify himself, it is recommended to take minimum of twice, two times bath in a day.
And rises early in the morning for devotional service.
Engaging in activities which will purify the dust of materialism which has.
So there are two kinds of dust or dirt.
One is which accumulates on our body.
Which we get purified of by taking our daily bath.
And there is another kind of dirt.
What is that kind of a dirt?
Like we talked about, root cause of our misery is our identification with the body.
So as we, just like we take our bath and then in course of the day we accumulate dirt, we perspire, we become dirty.
And then next day again we take a bath.
Likewise invariably we will get dirty as far as our consciousness is concerned.
So just like we all have got used to, if one day we don't take bath, we feel so uneasy, so uncomfortable, we feel so unclean.
Likewise we have to take spiritual bath everyday.
And what is that spiritual bath?
Start the day with devotional service.
Shravanam, Kirtanam, remembering the Lord, chanting the holy names.
There are many people who are very comfortable not taking a bath.
Maybe they take bath once in two days or three days.
Or they take bath sometime late in the evening.
They don't feel the necessity or probably they are quite comfortable without it.
Same is the case, a person may not feel the necessity of chanting, reading, hearing.
So naturally a devotee who is engaged in these activities, taking bath, purifying his consciousness on a regular basis, daily basis,
over a period of time he becomes cleansed both internally and externally.
A devotee is always expert because he has the complete overall picture of things.
He knows the purpose of this creation.
He knows why he has come in this world.
He knows from where he has come.
He knows where is he going to go after death.
And as a result, whatever activities he does, because the context is there, he is not lost.
He does that activity in knowledge, with the right context, with the right understanding.
As we started our discussion today, the root cause of our misery, even in the first chapter of the Gita, Arjuna was dilemma, confused, bewildered.
What will happen to my kinsmen?
And if we analyze, morally speaking, ethically speaking, all his arguments were quite wise.
But they were stemming from a very very deep rooted disease and that was identifying ourselves with the body.
And that's why in the second chapter of the Gita, Krishna says, you are speaking like a wise man.
There is no doubt about it.
You are speaking like a wise man.
But how come these impurities have come upon you?
To think that I am the body.
Therefore, without addressing the actual problems which Arjuna mentioned, should I fight, not fight, Krishna in the second chapter started talking about the soul.
In one sense, it looks out of context.
The conch shells have been blown.
And it's not an ordinary setting.
The battle is about life and death situation for crores and crores of people who have accumulated there.
And Krishna starts talking about the soul.
So for many of us also, the discussion what we have here may look something impractical.
We are dealing with real challenges in our world, in our lives.
And here we are talking about some philosophical concepts.
Soul, body, Krishna, life after death.
Actually all the challenges what we face, all the difficulties what we face is because of that root disease.
And the root disease is bodily concept of life.
Beginning of purification is when one starts understanding that I am not the body.
So because a devotee of his internal purification, because of his pure consciousness, because of pure knowledge,
he cultivates the understanding that I am not the body.
There may be ups and downs in the body.
There may be pain in the body.
But because he is not identified with the body, that pain does not touch him.
This is a very good example.
I have repeated n number of times.
Some of you would have got bored listening this.
Can anyone help me what example I am going to share?
Those who have been regular would have heard at least 5-6 times.
Like when I was young, I was very fond of cricket.
Now Indian team is in Australia.

And every ball would excite me or
What did he do? Why did he do that?
He should have been a little more patient.
Every ball was affecting my happiness and distress.
Because I was identifying with the body.
What Indian team was doing there was affecting my happiness and distress.
Now take it in right stride.
It's not that you cannot enjoy cricket or cannot watch cricket.
Now still Indian team is playing.
Still it is winning or losing.
But because I have not identified closely associated with that team.
But it is not affecting me.
Because you have identified with the team.
They win, you feel like you have won.
They lose, you feel you have lost.
Actually if you have not identified.
If you don't watch a cricket match.
Indian team is still win or lose.
But your happiness is unaffected by that.
So this is a crude example.
Now this is a story in the Bible.

There were profuse supply of water.

A snake which was living in that tree.
Hissed in the ears of Eve.
And said why don't you have an apple?
I am not supposed to have apple from this tree.
What is the harm if you have an apple?
You know the reason why you have been forbidden.
Why the Lord has told you not to have an apple?
Because if you have apple from this tree.
And God does not want others to become like him.
So this is the reason why.
This is the actual reason why the Lord has forbidden you to eat apple from this tree.
Also the Adam took that apple.
Anyone can help me with the answer.
They fell down from Garden of Eden to Eden Gardens in Calcutta.
So that is one thing which happened.
Fell in this world of duality.
Which is something very very similar which is there in our Vedic literature.
Why I cannot be the God?
This world is a world of duality.
So all what we are trying to do in this world is the world of duality.
We want happiness and we are trying to avoid pain. We want fame, avoiding infaming.
In the spiritual world, there is no duality. It's pure bliss.
So, he does not endeavor for material gains. A pure devotee does not endeavor for material gains.
Now, that does not mean that he is lazy, he is inert, has no aspirations, has no goals, has nothing to look forward to.
Not for his material gains. Whatever the Lord has given, he is satisfied.
Many of us are geared up to improve our material situation.
More money so that I can enjoy better. I can have a better house, a better car and things like that.
A pure devotee is callous about all these requirements.
But for the service of the Lord, he is very ambitious.
In other words, when it comes to building a palatial house for himself, he is not interested.
But when it comes to building a gorgeous temple for the Lord, he is very, very charged up.
The understanding is not that the goal has become to become desireless.
The goal is to have the right kind of desires.
Lust is desire to enjoy independent of the Lord.
And prema is to have desires to serve the Lord.
So, that way a devotee is not inert, he is not lazy, he is not, so to say, lazy.
One who neither grasps pleasure or grief, who neither laments nor desires,
and who renounces both auspicious and inauspicious things, is very dear to me.
A pure devotee is neither happy nor distressed over material gains or loss.
There may be some good times, there may be bad times.
There may be some gains, material gains.
There could be some material loss.
The body may go through some turbulence.
The example I gave, because he is disidentified with the body,
and is culturing the understanding that he is a soul,
he is unaffected by all these things.
If he loses anything which is very dear to him, he does not lament.
Because he has understanding of transient nature of this world,
has understanding that this is temporary.
I will share this example again.
